[34:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Results: Critical for Retention"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Importância crítica dos resultados, impacto de falhar em entregar resultados   [34:28] "The results critical. Huge emphasis on results in the client retention star. And as I mentioned, if you fail to get the result, obviously you're going to have a lot of refunds. You're going to have a lot of people that just drop."   [34:42] "And you got to take the feedback that comes. I see this most with marketing agencies and with info product businesses where they'll get a lot of feedback from people that can lead to dramatic improvement on their ability to generate results and they just ignore it."   [35:00] "You really want to ensure that you never ignore feedback that can improve your process to generating results faster that people are looking for."   [35:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Info Product Example: Master Internet Marketing"   NÚMEROS:  3-5 hours per class, dozens of hours of homework, 700 videos (High Ticket Agency)   VISUAL:  Exemplo de produto educacional, quantidade massiva de conteúdo, necessidade de digestibilidade   [35:15] "You know, like as an example, if you join into my info product, I have Master Internet Marketing, which is like no exaggeration, 3 to 5 hours per class with potentially dozens of hours of homework in between each class in video lessons."   [35:32] "So, what do we do? Well, we take the same topics and the lessons that are inside of that class and we break them down into SOPs that are more digestible. We take the same lessons that are inside of those classes and we break them out into smaller videos that they can watch."   [35:50] "We'll time code those lessons so they can fast forward specifically to the things or rewind and replay them when they need to go back and rewatch it again."   [36:00] "One piece of feedback that we got is like in High Ticket Agency as an example, there's like 700 fucking videos in there. I don't know in terms of hours how many hours worth of content there is, but I imagine without exaggerating it's in the high hundreds of hours. That's a lot of content."   [36:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Acting on Feedback"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Exemplos de implementação de feedback: SOPs, gravações, biblioteca organizada   [36:22] "So, I have to make it more digestible, you know, and that's one reason I produce SOPs all the time. Like what I'm literally talking to you about right now with the client retention star, I will send you the SOP for it so you can read through it after this in writing outside of what we're talking about here today right now."   [36:42] "So, you can reference back to it and not have to watch the video again. Does that make sense? That's an example of feedback that I received from students over the years that we've taken, implemented, and helped people get more results faster."   [37:00] "There was a time where I didn't record these lessons in order to incentivize people to show up live. Well, we got feedback from the inner circle students that this was just a terrible time for different people all over the world or they had something going on on Saturdays or they just didn't work on Saturday, you know, and they wanted to watch it."   [37:20] "You know, they didn't need to show up live to watch it and that's okay. So, we just started recording it, we send it in the group chat."   [37:30] "Now, we've gotten feedback recently that it'd be really nice to have like an organized library of all these weekly lessons and all historical mastermind talks. You know, that's on the list of things to get done to create that content library and organize it a particular way and then get you guys access to it."   [37:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Feedback Rule: One Person = Hundreds Thinking It"   NÚMEROS:  Hundreds to thousands of students   VISUAL:  Regra de feedback empresarial, uma reclamação representa muitos pensando o mesmo   [37:52] "When those kind of little things come up and they come up typically like once or twice, you know, they don't come up frequently out of hundreds and hundreds of people that I've sold - technically thousands to be clear over the years for our info products."   [38:08] "It's like we don't get that feedback very often, but we follow a good rule which is if one person says it, there's probably hundreds of people thinking it."   [38:18] "So, like when you get a little client feedback and it only comes from one person and you think like, 'Oh, it's only from one person. You know, it can't be that bad if only one person's saying it.' You're probably way wrong."   [38:32] "There's probably a lot of people who just aren't communicating it to you. They don't view it as worth their time to communicate. Sometimes you don't charge enough where people will give a fuck to communicate everything to you."   [38:45] "Sometimes you'll charge so much that everybody will communicate with you and attempt to get you to improve. You don't know where you're at in that spectrum. So, like I said, if you're getting a little feedback, take it - like there's a lot of people thinking it might just not be viewing it as worth telling you about."   [39:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Point #3: COMMUNICATION"   NÚMEROS:  Ponto 3 de 5   VISUAL:  Terceiro ponto da estrela destacado, comunicação como elemento fundamental   [39:08] "So, to be clear, results are critical. Now, this one, this is a really interesting part of the STAR in my opinion and it's communication."   [39:18] "Can I just ask one question about results?"   [39:20] "Yeah, sure. Ash, go ahead."   [39:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "ROI Discussion: ROAS + Fee"   NÚMEROS:  3:1 ROI, 5:1 ROI, 2:1 ROI (examples discussed)   VISUAL:  Discussão sobre métricas de ROI aceitáveis, ROAS incluindo fee da agência   [39:25] "Yeah. So basically it's just like on the lower end and I know you want to encourage us to go for the people who are earning more money but what would you say is an acceptable like ROI or ROAS plus fee - return on ad spend and fee. And what's an unacceptable like at the margin at the border?"   [39:48] "Well, why wouldn't you ask your client that and get the answer from them?"   [39:52] "Because they would manipulate the answer whereas you would have a better metric for me because it's nonbiased."   [40:00] "I don't think that's true at all. I think your client would tell you exactly what they want to hear. If you're not taking that feedback as honest, you gotta change your thinking up a little bit if that's the case."   [40:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Three-Tiered ROI System"   NÚMEROS:  Good, Great, Unacceptable (3 categories)   VISUAL:  Sistema de três níveis para estabelecer expectativas de ROI com clientes   [40:18] "If a client says, 'Okay, I have a desire to get, you know, like a 3 to 1 ROI or a 5 to 1 ROI.' And I always ask this question, Ash. I say okay what is - and this is like a three-tiered system I use - what is good, what is great, and what is unacceptable?"   [40:38] "That way I get three answers. So in your case with your ROAS like they might say okay great would be like anything above a 5 to 1. Good would be like a 3 to 1. Unacceptable would be like anything below a 2 to 1. Does that make sense?"   [40:55] "You don't necessarily want like a static answer out of this. Yeah. Got it. Like a ranged answer. And when you get the range it helps."   [41:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Applying Three-Tier System to All Metrics"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Aplicação do sistema de três níveis a múltiplas métricas: ROAS, cost per call, etc.   [41:08] "And we do this typically as an example with all statistics. So rather than just doing it with ROAS - like we will do it with ROAS to be clear. In addition to that, we'll do with things like cost per call where we're like, 'All right, so just in terms of expectations, what do you think is a good cost per call, a great cost per call, and like just a totally unacceptable? You wouldn't even put money towards it if it was that cost.'"   [41:32] "That helps us understand what the client thinks about the statistics before the statistics even occur. And it also helps us adjust the expectations."   [41:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Case Study: James - Cost Per Call Analysis"   NÚMEROS:  $450 cost per call, $15,000-$25,000 product, $5,000 downsell, $900 Google cost per call, 2:1 ROAS   VISUAL:  Exemplo real de cliente, análise de custo por call em contexto de produto high ticket   [41:45] "I've had clients before say some stuff like - I'll give you a great example right now. So, sitting here on this call right now is James. James sits at a $450 cost per call on average for his offer."   [42:00] "Put in the chat real quick. Is a $450 with these three answers - is a $450 cost per call good, great, or unacceptable? Put in the chat. What do you guys think? Good, great, unacceptable. 450 bucks. And that's a cost per qualified call to be clear. And by the way, they have a setter closer model."   [42:20] "What's the average lifetime and conversion rate though? Just base it on the cost per call. Is the cost per call $450 good, great, or unacceptable? Put in the chat."   [42:32] "So, Ash says unacceptable. I think it's okay, but it's not good. Adam says good. Drew says unacceptable. Good. Michael, Nick, you guys got answers? Mason."   [42:48] "So, to be clear, that will typically produce a great ROAS for them. That is, believe it or not, most likely in the good category of cost per call."   [43:00] "Now, I have that same client I just told you guys about where they have a $45 cost per call. Well, they have a $1,500 product. I mean, James' product's like $15 to $25,000. His lowest product - his downsell is like 5K. He has a really good close rate. He has a relatively good show rate."   [43:20] "You know, for James, a $450 cost per call - that's fine. I mean, that's good. It could be great if it was lower for sure. Unacceptable for them. I mean, guys, they have on Google at different times like $900 cost per call. It still flips a 2 to 1 ROAS."   [43:38] "So, it's like, yeah, I mean, you know, that's pushing it for sure. You don't want it to be up there, but I mean, hey, you could technically add another category in that example of like, well, I mean, it's still flipping a dollar into $2, you know, I guess that's okay. You know what I mean?"   [43:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Financial Models & Client Expectations"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Importância de modelos financeiros para estabelecer parâmetros operacionais   [44:00] "My point being, it's like these categories let individuals in the business know, you know, what parameters they need to operate within. And this is typically where financial models can come into play."   [44:12] "But as an example, it also just reveals more importantly the expectations in the client's head. So Ash, long story short, if you have a client that says, 'Okay, yeah, a great ROAS is like 20 to 1, a good ROAS is like 15 to 1, and unacceptable ROAS is like 10 to one.' And it's like, well, you know, they're fucked, you know."   [44:32] "And you know that they need to adjust those expectations. And if they fail to do so, then you know you'll have a problematic client before you even take the client on. These are questions that you ask in the sales process. Does that make sense?"   [44:45] "It does. And I think the biggest limiting barrier for me is charging a higher fee means I also have to charge them higher ad spend and get them to spend more and they may just have a low risk tolerance. Here in the UK I genuinely feel that's more the case, but I could be wrong."   [45:05] "So I mean I think the metric needs to include your fee, not just the ad spend return. You know what I mean?"   [45:12] "Of course it has to include your fee. It has to include - you're including it in the ROAS. Yeah. Yeah, of course. Got it. Got it."   [45:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "True ROAS Calculation"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Cálculo correto de ROAS incluindo todas as despesas: ad spend, fee, comissões de vendas, COGS   [45:22] "We'll include gross ROAS by advertisers - is usually manipulated to be whatever the amount of money came from the ads minus ad spend. It has to be minus your fee. Ideally, it's also minus any sales commissions that came in from closers."   [45:38] "Like, you have to get like a true ROAS. You know what I mean? As an example, it'd be like working with an e-com client and not factoring in their cost of goods and services, you know, their cost of goods sold."   [45:52] "It'd be like, you know, just ignoring the actual cost of operating the business and just saying, well, I mean, you know, you spent this much and we made this much, so that's the ROI. It's like, dude, that's not the furthest thing from true."   [46:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Communication = Expectations"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Comunicação como gestão de expectativas em múltiplas fases do relacionamento   [46:12] "So, to be clear, communication. This is in many forms and technically we could take what we just talked about there and we could throw that into the communication category because it is - I mean this example is in its finest form."   [46:30] "So, this just equals expectations once again. And expectations, they fall under many of the categories here that you'll see on the five-point client retention star, but communication happens in a few different places."   [46:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Four Types of Communication"   NÚMEROS:  4 tipos   VISUAL:  Comunicação no processo de vendas, onboarding, durante entrega de resultados, construção de relacionamento   [46:48] "Number one, it happens in the sales process. It obviously happens in the onboarding process. It happens in the deal, you know, once you're actually like in the process of generating results. So, like you're officially like in motion on whatever you're doing to help them produce."   [47:05] "It just happens also as well in like an ongoing basis more so from like a relationship perspective. We call this the relationship building part."   [47:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Relationship Building Example"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Exemplo de comunicação casual construindo relacionamento, piadas internas, interesses compartilhados   [47:18] "So, a good example of this, like I got a deal literally right now where this girl the other day instead of using the word 'another,' she accidentally sent the word 'panther.' I think it's a fucking joke. However, now she just like keeps randomly using like the panther emoji and it's like some kind of inside joke she's developed from making this communication mistake."   [47:40] "It's like I think it's stupid, but to be clear, I'll occasionally throw out this stupid joke at her and, you know, gets a good laugh out of her."   [47:50] "I have migraines. This girl has migraines. You know, yesterday I had to take a muscle relaxer. I had to take a steroid because I fucking threw my SI joint out. And it's all good though to be clear. But long story short, you know, I hit her up and I was like, 'Hey, like you know, just had to take a muscle relaxer. You ever take that with migraines?'"   [48:12] "It's like that's like ongoing communication that just like builds relationship stuff, you know, gets you - it gets you and the individual closer that you're working with."   [48:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "In-Deal Communication"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Comunicação focada em progresso, pequenas vitórias, melhorias em métricas   [48:25] "The in-deal communication, this is like as I mentioned like little wins, big wins. This is like communication towards the progress of the big win. These are like those little wins like cost per lead, you know, cost per sale, like funnel statistics, like just things improving overall."   [48:45] "As we talked about the onboarding communication is the emails that go out, the expectations that are set like when what is supposed to happen and meeting and exceeding those initial expectations, starting the relationship off strong."   [49:00] "Written communication, verbal communication, the expectation communications. A lot of the expectation communications are talked about most in the sales process. And it all starts there."   [49:15] "You kind of cascade down from sales process to onboarding to in the deal to like the ongoing relationship communication."   [49:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Case Study: Lost $200K/Month Client"   NÚMEROS:  $1.5-$2 million/month revenue, $1.1 million worst months, 6 months relationship building, $100-$200K/month lost   VISUAL:  História de perda de cliente high-value por falta de relacionamento, impacto de comunicação inadequada   [49:28] "Now, one mistake I've made through the years, and this is a big reason that this is on my client retention star system here is, look, you hear me say all the time, people just give a fuck about results. And that's true. That's extremely true."   [49:45] "However, if you don't have a great relationship with somebody, they will fire you eventually with somebody that they have a better relationship with that they view that can get them similar or the same results."   [50:00] "I know it kind of sounds silly, but I'll give you a great example. I had a client. We were making them so much fucking money. We were making them like $1.5, like $2 million a month, give or take the month. And our worst months were like $1.1 mil. We were doing great."   [50:20] "And I was in this phase. It was like, I don't know, 2019, 2020 during this specific window where I just really hate weekly calls. So like we just stopped doing all weekly calls with clients and like all our communication was just in writing like text message."   [50:38] "And you know like just written simply put - maybe an occasional voice note like if we needed to. We'd jump on like a short call. I wasn't doing anything you know just like build relationship type communication. It was just reporting communication and like update communication you know."   [50:58] "And things were going really well. We were getting paid a ton of money. They were getting paid a ton of money. And you'd think that like it's that simple, you know, and that's all that people care about."   [51:12] "And to be fair, I mean, once again, it really for the most part is - this relationship was like still a multi-year relationship. However, eventually, this person had gone somewhere. I don't know if it was an event or a mastermind. I don't know."   [51:28] "But they met a different advertiser who they just kind of like had a better relationship with, you know, and they didn't immediately like leave us for this other advertiser or anything like that."   [51:40] "It just, you know, this other person and this client of ours just kind of hit it off, you know, and they stayed in communication. They talked a lot more frequently than what we would talk on the phone, like verbally. They would just kind of like call each other, you know, and they were just building a relationship."   [52:00] "And maybe like I don't know, six months into this counterparty building a relationship with our client, we had I don't know just kind of like a rough patch with this specific client."   [52:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pandemic Impact on Client"   NÚMEROS:  $2 million/month → $1-1.3 million/month (40-50% drop)   VISUAL:  Impacto da pandemia nas receitas, momento de vulnerabilidade do cliente   [52:15] "Like these were the months where instead of hitting like $2 million a month, we were down to like a million to like, you know, $1.3, like somewhere in that range. And we were working to get the results back up. It wasn't anything crazy."   [52:32] "You know what happened was is there was a worldwide pandemic that shut the fucking earth down. And this particular client, they were still making a million to $1.3 million a month, but you know, up till that point, they were making $2 million a month."   [52:50] "And they were still radically convinced that they could make $2 million a month. So were we. But we were trying to work around like what the new messaging needed to be and just, you know, trying to adjust this specific client being geared towards like a pandemic related offer."   [53:08] "That way people would still buy it during this chaotic time in life. And the counterparty that was building a relationship with our client had reached out with like a plan."   [53:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Pivot Decision"   NÚMEROS:  Mesmo trabalho necessário, fee menor do competidor   VISUAL:  Cliente escolhendo competidor, mesmo escopo de trabalho, relacionamento como fator decisivo   [53:22] "Now, we reached out as well, obviously, with a plan, but keep in mind, we're just doing everything pretty much via text. So, our plan was well understood and it was not going to take long, but like, you know, we needed a bunch of stuff from the client. We needed a bunch of stuff redone. We needed a new VSL. We needed a bunch of new ads and like we just had to tweak damn near everything."   [53:45] "Now, how do you think the client looked at that? Well, the client looked at it like, okay, so these guys, you know, my revenue is essentially down like 50 to 60 - I'm sorry, 50 to 40% give or take the other month."   [54:00] "I got to refilm and redo everything anyway. And they got this other counterparty they've been talking to that's just essentially like, you know, if they ever do want to work together, he's got to do the same thing with them. He's got to refilm everything and just pretty much do everything that we're asking him to do anyway, but he could do it now with this counterparty."   [54:20] "So that was the choice he made. He was like, 'Hey, I can pay this other guy a little less than what I pay you. And long story short, I got to do everything that I got to do anyway for you with this other guy. Like, I'm going to take a stab with this other guy.'"   [54:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Lesson Learned: Relationship Matters"   NÚMEROS:  $100-200K/month lost   VISUAL:  Perda significativa devido à falta de relacionamento, lição sobre valor da comunicação casual   [54:38] "So essentially to save money and because once again the client viewed it like he was down 40-50%. And in addition to that we didn't really have like a strong relationship when you really look at it in hindsight you know."   [54:52] "We just had a really consistent set of results and like an occasional phone call. No voice notes really. Everything was just in writing via text message."   [55:05] "And that entire duration of time, the counterparty was in verbal communication with our client, like seeing them at different events throughout the year and just like I said, building a relationship with the individual."   [55:20] "Moral of the story is I lost like a hundred to almost damn near $200,000 a month off that deal based on my inability to have an ongoing relationship."   [55:32]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Basic Relationship Building"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Exemplos simples de construção de relacionamento: perguntar sobre família, viagens, interesses   [55:35] "Just like basic like, 'Hey man, how are your kids?' You know, like, 'Are you traveling this month?' Like, 'That's sweet, dude. How's that place?' You know, tell me about it."   [55:48] "Just telling them like - I still kind of think it's stupid shit to be clear. That's why I articulate it like this. But I mean, you know, like just little things, you know, just like little things that are insignificant to the deal that have like no value."   [56:05] "You know, things that I at that time would just devalue a lot because I was really trying to master efficiency at that time. So like this would play directly against my be efficient as efficient as I can in every deal."   [56:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Evolution of Perspective"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Mudança de mentalidade sobre relacionamentos, maturidade empresarial   [56:22] "Today, like in today's day and age, I - you know, believe it or not, I know it kind of sounds crazy, but I do genuinely give a fuck about these like little things in my client's life, not just for the monetary gain that I'm going to get as a result of giving a fuck about it."   [56:40] "But I don't know. I feel like my brain's just developed a little more. You know, I'm older now. I recognize that like, you know, this is obviously why we all make money is like pretty much for the little things that we get to go experience in life."   [56:55] "And it's cool, you know, to hear some of the cool little things. Like I have clients that like will go and buy new properties or like buy new stuff or like put their kids in certain schools or like go like travel places I've never heard of."   [57:12] "And it's cool to just get more perspective about how life is for other people too. And simply put, dude, in my world, when somebody's paying you like a hundred to 200K a month or like tens of thousands of dollars a month and you're getting to that point."   [57:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "High-Value Client Relationship Reality"   NÚMEROS:  $100-200K/month clients   VISUAL:  Necessidade de relacionamento genuíno com clientes high-value, impossibilidade de ser apenas transacional   [57:32] "I assure you from firsthand experience, you fail to build like an actual relationship with those individuals and you solely look at it like it's just a result relationship and that's it. I mean, dude, nobody really wants to pay somebody $100-200K a month they don't have an actual relationship with them."   [57:50] "And soon as you get away from that, like deals get pretty fucked pretty fast."   [58:00] "Now, communication comes in many different forms. It's not just like ongoing basis communication like the story I just told. It's just as I mentioned like the in-deal communication like the momentum communication, the progress communication."   [58:15] "And obviously you skip over the sales process and the onboarding communication as soon as you get into the deal, but those little things like you can't ignore those little things."   [58:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Don't Stuff Communication Into Weekly Calls"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Aviso contra concentrar toda comunicação em calls semanais, necessidade de comunicação contínua   [58:32] "Don't just stuff all the communication into a weekly call. You know, don't just like ignore your clients."   [58:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Case Study: 'Feeling Invisible'"   NÚMEROS:  2 years (Inner Circle member), 1 month delay (ticket response)   VISUAL:  Exemplo de cliente se sentindo invisível, impacto de pequenos atrasos na comunicação   [58:42] "I had an inner circle student like two months ago who's been in the inner circle for like two years. He's come to almost every mastermind and he hit me up and he was like, you know, he wanted to buy a ticket for one of his staff members."   [59:00] "And I think it was - I don't know if it was Leo who had just failed to communicate it to me or if I had just forgot, but we didn't send the guy the ticket link to buy the extra ticket for his staff for like a month."   [59:15] "And obviously there's no rush because like he doesn't need to buy it right away, you know? You just need to buy it before the mastermind. So anyway, we didn't look at that as a big deal at all, but he hit me up and he was like, 'Hey, you know, I want to drop out.'"   [59:32] "And we're like, 'Why?' And he was like, you know, like, 'What? What's wrong?' And he was like, 'Well, I don't know, man. Like, I can't even give you money anymore. You know, I just kind of feel invisible.'"   [59:48] "And I was like, 'What? Like, what do you mean you feel invisible, dude? Like, I talk to you a lot, you know? Like, what are you talking about?'"   [60:00] "And it was a rather emotional response from the guy that we got. And I'm not necessarily sure that it holds true to be fair. But regardless, that was the word that we had gotten back from somebody who had wanted to drop out was that they had felt invisible."   [60:18] "And that was terrible feedback to hear from somebody that we, you know, in our world, you know, we did genuinely give a fuck about the guy. We give a fuck about all of you guys progressing and getting results."   [60:32] "And I sure hope that you guys understand that I do care about whatever the fuck's going on in your life, you know, and that we want to see you progress, that will help you do that in any way we can."   [60:45] "But that was terrible feedback to hear, you know. So, we made a lot of adjustments since then in an attempt to just make people feel seen, you know, and really go out of the way regardless of what we think."   [61:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Taking Feedback Seriously"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Importância de levar feedback a sério, fazer as pessoas se sentirem valorizadas   [61:02] "When you get feedback like that, like you have to take that shit seriously. And it all falls under that umbrella of clearly there was some kind of communication fault when a guy tells you he feels fucking invisible and then he can't even give you money anymore."   [61:18] "You get what I'm saying? Something as simple as just not sending a guy an invoice within like a day of him asking can lead that individual to think I'm invisible, you know, or I'm not valued. You get what I'm saying?"   [61:32] "Like think about this guys. All the people who give you money - like you want to make them feel good about the fact that they give you money, you know? You got to show that you actually value the relationship."   [61:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Communication Without Words"   NÚMEROS:  10-11 years ago   VISUAL:  Exemplo de comunicação não-verbal, impacto emocional de gestos inesperados   [61:48] "So, as I mentioned, you do that in many ways, but all of it falls under the umbrella of communication. And I can communicate things without having to use words."   [62:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Frank Kern Cookies Story"   NÚMEROS:  10-11 years ago   VISUAL:  História de impacto emocional de presente inesperado, cookies como gesto de cuidado   [62:02] "I always use this example of Frank Kern back in the day. To be clear, I bought some random product from Frank where he sent you like a physical book every month. And this is like 10, 11 years ago."   [62:18] "And within the first month of being subscribed to this, I get a random delivery that came in a box. And that was not the shape of the usual folder that would come in the mail that had the book in it. And I don't even think it was labeled from Frank."   [62:35] "It just had a note in it with some cookies. And it just said, 'Hope you're having a great day. Here's some cookies to make it better. Frank Kern.'"   [62:48] "And this was like one of the first months of me being a business owner, dude. And I was - I don't know. I mean, it was pretty intense window for sure. Just felt like I was getting the fucking kicked out of me all the time."   [63:02] "And I remember that day so vividly, dude. Those cookies showed up. Silly as it sounds, they meant a lot to me. You know, I was like, damn, dude. Felt good, you know?"   [63:15] "And he didn't have to say anything other than just such a simple - that was communicated to me in a way that created like an emotional bond between me and this person who has no idea I even exist."   [63:30] "You know, just something that's likely automated, but it did a lot for me. Does that make sense?"   [63:38] "Okay, cool. Anybody have questions on the communication umbrella?"   [63:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "James' Customer Retention Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  1 hour (response time), 72 hours (follow-up), 1 week (gift), 30 days (personalized gift)   VISUAL:  Sistema detalhado de onboarding e retenção em múltiplos touchpoints temporais   [63:45] "I got a couple of additional comments maybe I could share that seems to be working well in our customer retention strategy."   [63:52] "Please."   [63:55] "So we read this book called Never Lose a Customer Again by a guy called Joey Coleman and our executive team just read it all and studied it and discussed it."   [64:08] "And as a result of that plus, you know, other things over the years that we've picked up. We - as soon as a client becomes a client, we process their credit card. We internally go, we're on the clock. We got one hour starting from the moment the credit card gets processed to wow them."   [64:28] "And so right away our onboarding manager reaches out to the client to schedule a time to get them on an onboarding call. I get a notification to record a personalized welcome video to the client."   [64:42] "So, I when I get a sale notification, first of all, I'm going fucking yeah. And then secondly, I wait about five minutes to get a little bit of background on the member."   [64:55] "And it's basically the salesman's notes that say John Smith, 51, lives in Phoenix, drinks five drinks a night, wants to save his marriage."   [65:08] "So, I read that and then I quickly record a selfie video and I go, 'Hi, John Smith. James Swanwick here. Wanted to welcome you to our community. Great to have you with us. I understand that you're out there in Phoenix and you're drinking four drinks at night at the moment and you really wanted to quit back so you can save your marriage. I love your vision. Just wanted to welcome you. Blah blah blah, etc.'"   [65:32] "Then I save that video to a software called Streamable. And then I put that link to the onboarding manager and then she texts it to the client and say, 'Hey, John, our founder James, just wanted to welcome you. We recorded this video for you.'"   [65:50] "And so, usually within like a few hours, the new client gets that personalized video and the client usually responds, 'Wow, amazing. So cool. Incredible that I got that.'"   [66:05] "And then we always just make sure that we do what we tell them we're going to do. So, salesman says, 'You'll get a call from the onboarding manager within the hour.' We make sure we do. 'On that call, you'll do this, this, and this.' We make sure we do."   [66:20] "So we just say we're going to be men and women of our word. We just do exactly what we promised. We give them a personalized video. And then within 72 hours, the onboarding manager calls them again just to check in. Have you got everything? Is everything okay? Any concerns?"   [66:38] "And then within a week, we send them a pair of Swannies blue light blocking glasses, which is my other company. We send them a gift."   [66:48] "And then within the first 30 days, we try to send them a personalized gift. So, based on what their hobbies are, if they like the Denver Broncos, we get them a Denver Broncos coffee mug or something or a t-shirt. If they're into, I don't know, whatever, crystals, we send them crystals and we try to do that within 30 days."   [67:10] "And it's created enormous goodwill, a big wow factor. And most importantly, it's almost extinguished our cancellable refund requests. We still get a few, but I would submit probably half of what we used to get just by that attention to detail in the first hour, the first 72 hours, the first week, and the first 30 days."   [67:32] "Hope that helps."   [67:35] "Hell yeah. Oh, great feedback. Thank you. Great book recommendation as well. All right, let's jump into these last two."   [67:42] "What was the book?"   [67:45] "Sorry. Go ahead."   [67:48] "Never Lose a Customer Again by Joey Coleman. See, look. Thank you for that recommendation, James."   [67:55] "Sure. Thank you."   [68:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Point #4: REPORTING"   NÚMEROS:  Ponto 4 de 5   VISUAL:  Quarto ponto da estrela destacado, reporting como feedback loop crítico   [68:02] "Great input as well. Okay, so to be clear, no matter what the product and service is, in most instances, you have something that you can report, especially nowadays, the amount of sensors, the amount of tech, the amount of analytics that are available to us for whatever it is that we're doing can tremendously improve our retention with clients when they know that they're actually generating some kind of result with it."   [68:30] "How does somebody know that they're generating a result? With some type of feedback that can come from data."   [68:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Data Visibility Requirements"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Necessidade de dados que sejam: verdadeiros, precisos, confiáveis, e que forneçam feedback loop   [68:42] "Clients need visibility into data that they agree is true and accurate and that in addition to that they can trust and that gives them some type of feedback loop."   [68:55] "So when it comes to reporting, this is in my opinion one of the most undermined like tech uses that are available to us nowadays when it comes to this reporting feedback cycle."   [69:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Eight Sleep Analytics Trust"   NÚMEROS:  10% variance (HRV comparison with Whoop)   VISUAL:  Comparação de analytics entre devices, construção de confiança nos dados   [69:12] "So back to the Eight Sleep mattress. The first two nights the analytics honestly kind of shocked me. Like I looked at what it claimed my HRV was and I was like holy shit like my HRV is awesome."   [69:28] "And I looked at my Whoop band and it was like within 10% of that, but it was a little higher than my Whoop band showed. And I was like, man, maybe I have a higher HRV than what my Whoop band's been telling me. That's pretty cool."   [69:45] "You know, I really have strived - spent a lot of time and money on getting my HRV up. And it had showed my REM sleep and my deep sleep. And that was about the same as what it showed on my Whoop band."   [70:00] "And that right away gave me a lot of confidence in the reporting of that specific new tech that I just bought. And it let me trust the fact that, you know, maybe this thing is more accurate than my Whoop band or maybe some of these analytics that I'm getting are going to give me like a different perspective on my sleep that I haven't gotten before from another analytic tool."   [70:22] "And it made me feel a lot better about the purchase right away. I'll tell you that. It showed my breathing rate was a little lower and it gave me additional data into just body temperature and I thought that was really interesting. It was a new category of analytic that opened up to me and this little feedback loop."   [70:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "When Reporting Breaks: Night 3"   NÚMEROS:  1.5 hours (incorrect sleep tracking), Night 3   VISUAL:  Exemplo de falha no reporting, impacto na confiança quando dados são incorretos   [70:45] "It kind of got broken a little bit on the third night. On the third night I have no idea what happened but it just really fucking whiffed on the feedback. It told me I had only slept like an hour and a half, which obviously I didn't."   [71:00] "And it fucked up every other statistic that followed, you know, as a result of only tracking an hour and a half of sleep. So, it gave me a lot of uncertainty to say the least."   [71:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Marketing Agency Reporting Discrepancies"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Exemplo de discrepâncias entre plataformas de analytics: Ads Manager vs Calendly vs CRM   [71:15] "Sometimes as a marketing agency when I reach out to a client, I'm like, 'Hey, like we just got, you know, this many calls and we're just using like the ads manager to report that statistic, you know, and they'll be like, you know, like I don't know. I think we got a little less than that.'"   [71:32] "And then I'm like, 'Okay, cool. Where are you looking?' And they're like, 'Oh, I'm looking in here like in Calendly or like whatever.' And then I'm like, 'Okay, well, so to be clear, you don't think Facebook's accurate? I don't think Calendly is accurate.'"   [71:48] "'Where can we both look that's going to have an accurate example? What if I pull a CRM report real quick? Everybody who's come through this funnel has to have this tag and this funnel is only being used for us, right?' And they're like, 'Yeah.'"   [72:05] "I'm like, 'All right, great. Let's go into the CRM and let's look there.' And then we get this different number that's different from Calendly, that's different from the ads manager. And I'm like, do you agree that this one's true? And they're like, yeah. And I'm like, all right, cool. We'll use this from here forward."   [72:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Critical Reporting Rule"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Regra fundamental: ambas as partes devem acreditar que os dados são precisos   [72:25] "I need to have an analytic source that both the client and I agree is accurate. I can't believe it's accurate, and the client can't believe it's inaccurate. That's a discrepancy in reporting."   [72:38] "And the reporting is totally void and useless if the client does not believe it's true. So, I want you to understand something real quick. Even if the analytic that you are reporting you know to be true, if the client does not believe it to be true, it is not effective and it will not work. It needs to be changed."   [73:00] "It has to be something once again that the client believes is true."   [73:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Triangulating the Truth"   NÚMEROS:  3 different analytic tools   VISUAL:  Método de usar três ferramentas diferentes para validar dados e encontrar a verdade   [73:08] "So this is in our world typically why we do this concept called triangulating the truth. We'll have typically three different analytic tools for our reporting in our marketing agency."   [73:20] "For our students, as an example, like each student has a different type of result that they're looking to generate. Sometimes it's like new clients, sometimes it's x amount more dollars in total revenue."   [73:35] "Sometimes it's things like this where they just want to retain clients for longer. Like I need some type of analytic that I can also tie to the progress that the client is making with our product and service. I need something simply put that just demonstrates that they're moving forward."   [73:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Reporting vs Results Distinction"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Diferença entre categoria de resultados e categoria de reporting, métricas vs benchmarking   [73:55] "This is critical. This is a little different than the results category because this is how you actually analytically benchmark the result happening."   [74:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "James' Sobriety Tracking Example"   NÚMEROS:  Drinks per day/week, hangover frequency, positive interactions   VISUAL:  Exemplo de métricas quantificáveis para rastrear progresso em parar de beber   [74:08] "So like with what James is just talking about where he's helping people stop drinking - like dude they have to be able to analytically look at their life and say like yes the quantity of drinks I've consumed per day or per week is quantifiably gone down."   [74:25] "You know the amount of quantifiable better days I've had have gone up. You know what I mean? The amount of hangovers I've had have gone down. The amount of like, you know, positive and successful interactions with my wife since I don't want to lose my wife have gone up."   [74:45] "You know what I mean? Like the amount of bad days for whatever it is that they're trying to overcome have gone down. And when and if these people become questionable if James' service is actually working for them, they need to be able to literally look back at some type of analytic."   [75:02]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Personal HRV Journey"   NÚMEROS:  10-12x improvement (HRV over 6-7 years)   VISUAL:  Exemplo pessoal de tracking de longo prazo, validação de investimento em saúde   [75:05] "And be like, well, yeah, look, so as an example, like if James had a journal, you know, or some type of log, and I'm sure he does, where people are literally inputting this type of data into it and it demonstrates to them in a way they could look back on through time."   [75:22] "Like I remember when I very first got my Whoop band, like my performance coach and I thought it was broken from how fucked my stats were. Like my HRV was literally the range of a hundred year old man. Like we thought it was fucked. We didn't believe it."   [75:40] "And dude, when I look back at that same data, you know, like 6 to 7 years later, and my HRV is literally 10 to 12x what it was back then, right in the range of where I should be - technically a little younger of a man than I am right now."   [76:00] "Dude, that's the type of fucking analytic that lets me know all the time, energy, and money I've spent on my recovery and my health has been worth it."   [76:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Medical Intervention Comparison"   NÚMEROS:  $700 (doctor visit), $900 (chiropractor), pain 6-7 → 1-2   VISUAL:  Exemplo de comparação quantitativa entre dois tratamentos, dados guiando decisões futuras   [76:15] "You know, like - like I said, me yesterday. I spent $700 to get a doctor to come over to my house and inject me in the ass with a fucking muscle relaxer and a steroid because I had thrown my back out, bending down to get into a freezer, my SI joint on my left side."   [76:35] "It's like your left hip on your back essentially got thrown out. And I've had that issue now for about two, three years where like just randomly I'll be doing the most basic shit and I'll throw it out."   [76:50] "And it's generally because my hamstrings are really tight. When I go through periods of time where I'm not just stretching the fucking shit out of my hamstrings, my left SI joint could get thrown out."   [77:05] "So, long story short, yesterday, once again, $700 towards a doctor, injectables, took my pain down from like a fucking six, seven down to like a one to two."   [77:18] "You know, there was a lot of like lower quality of life on whatever it was. Not last night, but the night before. Like just basic things I couldn't do because my back was thrown out."   [77:32] "And my chiropractor came over. Oh, that fucked me up so much worse. I spent $900 on my chiropractor to come over. That made the problem like no joke, like three times worse."   [77:48] "I'm like highly questionable on whether I'm ever going to have him come over again on issues like that. Like it seems like the things I do get quantifiably benefit - the things that I do benefit from that are quantifiable with my chiropractor are not things like that apparently, just like maintenance mode stuff."   [78:08] "But with the doctor, it's like, dude, the doctor quantifiably on a reporting basis took my pain down a tremendous amount, like loosened my back up again, and enabled me to be able to stretch and just do the basic stuff I needed to do with my hamstrings without pain. It helped a ton."   [78:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Reporting Enables Service Evaluation"   NÚMEROS:  Dozens of positive experiences vs 1 negative   VISUAL:  Capacidade de olhar retrospectivamente para avaliar serviços, balanceando experiências   [78:32] "Now, my point being, this is how people judge the results that you're generating and the experience that they get with your product or service is the reporting itself."   [78:45] "So, if I ever go question Dr. [name] and my chiropractor. Long story short, I'm going to be able to think of that exact experience and it's going to make me think less of the service."   [79:00] "But I have to remember that there are dozens and dozens and dozens of times where, you know, he's snapped me up and helped me with the adjustments that he makes to my back that have helped me a tremendous amount."   [79:15] "Now, my point being, if I have an emphasis on the bad experiences I've had, and I don't have the ability to look back at the great experiences I've had with that product or service, just like I had with the Eight Sleep mattress, the third night, the third night statistics were fucked."   [79:32] "Well, the other, you know, nine or 10 days that I've had it now, they've all been very accurate. I haven't had that issue since. So, maybe I just didn't sleep on that side of the bed. Maybe I was like on Marina's side of the bed. I don't know."   [79:48] "But long story short, like you know, nine out of 10 days have been good. One out of 10 days have been bad. So my point being, it's like this is what gives us and our clients and the people that work with us a way to look back and feel good about what they've invested into."   [80:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Reporting Can Work Against You"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Aviso sobre como métricas ruins podem perder clientes, importância do balanço positivo   [80:12] "Now, this can also obviously play against you. If there's too many bad things and not enough good things, that's what will lose you deals. This is what will lose business more than anything else is if those analytics and those quantifiable metrics are not in your favor."   [80:30] "So, it's a good benchmark to be able to increase the score towards retaining the client rather than losing the client. And it's not just the usage for the person who bought, it's also the usage of your company."   [80:45] "Those analytics are critical for you to be able to see what needs improved upon as well. This is a feedback loop that comes from numbers. It's not just for the client. Once again, it's for you as well in the company. Does that make sense?"   [81:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Team Training on Data Sharing"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Desafio de treinar equipe sobre quando compartilhar dados, timing correto   [81:02] "How do you make sure that like your team is trained on knowing when to share data and when not to? Sometimes my team have shared data at the wrong time, bad data, and I tried to train them."   [81:18] "Maybe I just need to hire smarter people, but like they don't seem to get that importance of regularly reporting back small wins, which is the communication, and some of them don't seem to report the right data at the right time."   [81:32] "Most likely a training issue. I'd encourage you step in and do those kinds of things yourself. That way you have examples that you can like put into a training document and like you know walk through it with them and show them what they're supposed to do."   [81:48] "You can also do a simple system where they report what they're going to say to you or somebody in the organization first to kind of like approve it prior to it going out to the client if you don't have that trust with those staff yet."   [82:02] "Yeah, that's a good idea. And do you use Slack for that?"   [82:08] "You use whatever the hell you want. I prefer Telegram, but you know, it's just my personal preference. Whatever yours is, just do whatever you want."   [82:18] "Gotcha."   [82:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Point #5: ADDED VALUE"   NÚMEROS:  Ponto 5 de 5   VISUAL:  Quinto e último ponto da estrela, valor adicionado como elemento surpresa   [82:22] "Okay, cool. All right, last category in the client retention star and then we'll get out of here, is added value."   [82:32] "So, as an example for what Ash was talking about earlier, he had a client that claimed they wanted to build a funnel. Ash, walk us through the situation again. I don't want to put words in your mouth."   [82:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Ash's Concession Example"   NÚMEROS:  6 weeks (month 1), 7 weeks (actual), 2 concessions   VISUAL:  Exemplo de valor adicionado através de concessões, flexibilidade com cliente   [82:48] "Basically, I gave him a concession, which was that month one would be six weeks instead of 4 weeks because there was a lot of research and investigation that needed doing and there was a handover from another agency."   [83:05] "He signed the contract and he said I'll pay now and then he said but let's start end of July cuz I want to build out the landing pages before you get started."   [83:18] "Then because of some internal problems on their end his developer was on holiday. They delayed in building out the landing pages. So, by the time we start the six week term one, the landing pages aren't ready."   [83:32] "And two, he then said, 'Oh, by the way, also like I messed up the handover with the previous agency and the dates. So, I know you said in weeks one and two of the first six week month, you are not going to do any ads. You're just going to prepare, but I need someone to sort of like make sure these ads, the existing ads continue to run well before we switch to your advertising strategies.'"   [83:58] "And so I was like I made some concessions and I said look if it's going to help we'll make a few optimizations in these first two weeks and we'll push back week one by another week which meant we end up basically doing we're ending up basically doing seven weeks in month one instead of six."   [84:18] "So, I gave him two concessions and it was just annoying because not because I don't mind giving added value, but because I typically overgive and then the client gets used to me overgiving in the past anyway."   [84:32] "And that has turned out sometimes to my disadvantage in being able to renegotiate terms later down the line. So, I'm kind of glad that Jeremy you said that actually it's a good thing to give added value, but maybe we can talk about also how you don't get exploited by the client either."   [84:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Added Value Definition"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Valor adicionado em múltiplas formas, alguns elementos correlacionam com comunicação   [84:52] "Good question. Some of these things correlate to the other points in the star." I didn't sign up to get a thing of cookies. It was an unexpected small gift that, you know, at that particular stage in my life made my day and it was awesome and it showed up like exactly what I wanted it to. It was tremendous." [85:52] "Noticed he said, you know, if the person likes the Denver Broncos, the football team, he's not necessarily just going to go buy them tickets or, you know, like an autographed thing. He's just going to get them something small like a mug, you know, just doesn't take a lot to give somebody the wow factor."   [86:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Master Internet Marketing Added Value"   NÚMEROS:  $50 million (Google Ads spend by Chason), 60+ videos (course)   VISUAL:  Exemplo de adicionar curso completo sem custo extra, colaboração com expert   [86:15] "Added value can also come like as an example, in our Master Internet Marketing group, we have the seven weeks of live classes and each week is an individualized topic. And in week four, we talked about Google Adwords."   [86:32] "And recently, one of our inner circle students, Chason, he crossed a threshold on Google Adwords where he had spent over $50 million on Google Adwords. Chason had also came out with a Google Adwords course."   [86:48] "And I had asked Chason, I was like, 'Hey man, do you want to throw this course into the Master Internet Marketing group and I can pull out the existing Google Adwords homework library and I can just throw your entire course in there. I think that would add more value to the group.'"   [87:05] "Chason was like, 'Hell yeah, dude. I'd love to do that.' I then hit up all the students and I tell him, I'm like, 'Hey guys, what we're going to do is we're going to have Chason here who spent, you know, $50 million on Google AdWords profitably. We're going to get him and his entire like, you know, 60 plus video course added in here within like a week. You know, I'll ping you guys as soon as it's done.'"   [87:30] "You know, obviously the students fucking love that. Like I'll film new stuff all the time that we didn't originally sell with the course that the students have no expectation of getting and we just do it, you know, we don't charge them more for it. We just add the fucking value."   [87:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "KEY PRINCIPLE: Not Expected Value"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Princípio fundamental do valor adicionado: deve ser surpresa, não esperado   [87:52] "And this is a very key thing I want you to understand about the added value section. It is not expected. That is what makes this category special. I say that again. The added value category is not expected value."   [88:08] "It is literally added value that is random, a surprise. Just goes above and beyond to show that you give a fuck. It's all it is. It's as literally as simple as that."   [88:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Core Question of Added Value"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Pergunta fundamental para guiar valor adicionado   [88:25] "What can you do for the people who give you money that they don't expect you to do that demonstrate you give a fuck about them? That's it. Literally as simple as that."   [88:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Added Value Examples Summary"   NÚMEROS:  $1,000 (donation example), $80-90K/month (client spend)   VISUAL:  Múltiplos exemplos de valor adicionado: cookies, vídeos, cursos, doações   [88:42] "For Frank Kern, it was cookies. For James, it's a personalized video and a fucking something about their hobby, you know? For me, it's I'll do whatever I can to go above and beyond and and provide that added value to my student groups, my clients."   [89:00] "You know, sometimes it's as simple - like I saw the other day one of my clients, they had a - in one of their son's classmates was doing a fundraiser and the guy posted about it on his story. I donated a thousand bucks, you know, and I didn't even tell the client."   [89:18] "Client hit me up. I was like, 'Hey, man. I saw that you donated a thousand dollars to this shit. That's insane. I deeply appreciate you doing that. Thank you.' And I didn't even tell them, you know, they knew they were tracking it."   [89:32] "But my point is like, dude, just little stuff like that, you know, just shows you give a fuck. And it could be in many ways. Doesn't have to be expensive. Doesn't have to be $1,000. But that particular client, just to be clear, they spend upwards of like $80 to $90K a month with us currently. So, we're happy to give back $1,000."   [89:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Added Value Should Be Proportional"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Valor adicionado deve ser proporcional ao que o cliente está pagando   [89:55] "Anyway, my point is is like, look, relative to the amount of money that somebody spending with you should be relative to the amount of added value you could provide them."   [90:08] "For Ash, it was a concession on two different things where he technically worked a week more than he should have and did a few extra things. It's what the client doesn't expect, but you do anyway."   [90:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "5-Point Client Retention Star SOP"   NÚMEROS:  5 pontos completos   VISUAL:  Resumo visual do sistema completo, SOP será enviado   [90:25] "To be clear, there's an entire five-star client retention SOP that I'll send over with this recording after it's processed and uploaded into the group."   [90:35] "Does anybody else have anything they want to add to this? Any kind of things that have worked with you that you guys want to talk about real quick? Any questions? Feel free to unmute yourself."   [90:48] "You got anything? I just had a question about your onboarding, Jeremy. Is there anything like special you do or any - I guess any anything that we should know about in your onboarding process with specifically agency clients?"   [91:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Jeremy's Agency Onboarding: No Questionnaires"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Estratégia de não usar questionários longos com clientes ocupados e ricos   [91:08] "Yeah, I'll give you a good one. You know how some people make people fill out like a dock or some shit with a bunch of questions in it? Yeah. We don't do that. We work with busy rich people and I've never met anybody that likes to fill out long questionnaires when they go into a client relationship. So, we don't do anything like that."   [91:32]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Only Charge When Ads Go Live"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Estratégia de não cobrar por trabalho preparatório, apenas quando ads estão rodando   [91:35] "In addition to that, one thing that we've - like Ash was just saying this, which is a good example. Way back when we first started our agency, we thought we deserve to get paid for everything that we did."   [91:50] "And like I said, eventually down the line, I learned that all people really give a fuck about is results. So instead of ever charging somebody to like build a funnel out or like you know tell them what to say in their ad videos or even like editing the ad videos, we only charge once we actually have the ads live in the ads manager."   [92:12] "So as an example, some of our onboarding processes now include like getting on a call and telling the client what to say in a VSL, like scripting that out with them. It includes like here's the ad ideas that we need you to go shoot, like go film that stuff. It includes like building a little funnel for whatever we're going to drive the traffic to."   [92:32] "And we won't charge them anything for that because none of that drives the result. That's just stuff that needs done to get the result. And to be clear, we found that we would you know just have a lot of pissed off people if we would charge them for those things and then you know like charge them again whatever amount like you know 30 days after that."   [92:52] "To be clear sometimes clients take longer than 30 days to do that basic shoot you know sometimes they take like a week sometimes they take two weeks sometimes it takes a month you know we don't know. So, long story short, we do things like that upfront and we consider that a part of our onboarding process."   [93:12] "And long story short, we only charge them when we actually go into the ads manager and press publish."   [93:20] "Right. So, you get them to sign a contract and then 30 to 45 days later if they're delayed on getting it to you, you'll then charge them."   [93:30] "Yeah, that's right. Yeah."   [93:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Dentist/Medical Reporting Challenge"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Desafio específico de rastrear pacientes que vieram de ads em contexto médico   [93:38] "And have you - you know, you said you'd consulted medical as well, Jeremy. One of the biggest hardest things there is getting the dentists to tell you how many patients actually move forward."   [93:52] "I mean for the high ticket cosmetic cases is one thing but also like how many patients actually came in from the Google ads including the calls and the leads and that makes it sometimes very ambiguous with SEO."   [94:08] "It's even worse. We do amazing SEO work for some of our clients but they can see that there's an additional traffic driven to their website. But going back to this point about results, which is so pivotal, like we can't truly measure the impact we're having, and sometimes they're even not fully transparent with us."   [94:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Solutions for Tracking Medical Patients"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Múltiplas soluções: traffic conversion rates, offline conversion reporting, CRM data, unique funnels, call tracking   [94:32] "Well, that's a feedback cycle that you've got to consider. So, right now, the way that you're attempting to agree on a result happening is where the issue is. You have to be able to adjust the way that you're attempting to agree on a result."   [94:48] "And to be clear, you have to have a way that successfully, you know, establishes that results are happening from your service if you want to retain clients for longer durations of time. You can't say - it could be traffic. Yeah, it could be anything."   [95:05] "I mean, to be clear, it could just be okay. So, for SEO, as an example, if their average website conversion is X, you know, then to be fair, X amount of people come in from the total website traffic, then that's the default statistic you're going to use as long as the client agrees to it."   [95:25] "For ads, it could be like offline conversion reporting where you export their payment data, import it into the ads managers, and look at the offline conversion reporting. You know, like there's plenty of ways to accomplish what you're attempting to say you can't accomplish."   [95:42] "Gotcha."   [95:45] "To be clear, CRM data as well, you know, unique funnels, like there's plenty of ways - call tracking on click to call buttons. You know, having specific phone numbers on their website that analytically report back who called, you know, like there's all kinds of fucking you can do for them. You're just not adjusting." [97:52] "Cool. All right, everybody. Talk soon. Bye. Bye."     📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS: 92% taxa de retenção média de clientes $6,652 custo total Eight Sleep $1.5-2 milhões/mês gerados para cliente perdido $100-200K/mês perdidos por falta de relacionamento $450 custo por call (cliente James) $45 custo por call (cliente lead gen) $50 milhões gastos em Google Ads (Chason) 10-12x melhoria em HRV ao longo de 6-7 anos 700+ vídeos no High Ticket Agency 3-5 horas por aula no Master Internet Marketing $700 (visita médica) vs $900 (quiroprático) $1,000 doação para fundraiser de cliente $80-90K/mês gasto por cliente high-value 1 hora (tempo de resposta de James) 72 horas (follow-up) 30 dias (presente personalizado) October 19-20 (Q4 Mastermind)   ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:   5-Point Client Retention Star: Onboarding  - Expectativas definidas e cumpridas Results  - Pequenas vitórias → Grande vitória Communication  - 4 tipos: vendas, onboarding, in-deal, relacionamento Reporting  - Analytics confiáveis e acordadas mutuamente Added Value  - Surpresas não esperadas   Onboarding Best Practices: Definir expectativas claras desde o início Comunicar todos os detalhes de entrega Underpromise, overdeliver Não cobrar por trabalho preparatório Apenas cobrar quando ads estão live   Results Communication: Comunicar pequenas vitórias constantemente Benchmarks: primeiro lead, primeiros 5, 10, 25, 50 Feedback loop com equipe de vendas Transformar problemas em oportunidades de comunicação Criar momentum através de vitórias acumuladas   Communication Framework: Sales process communication Onboarding communication In-deal communication (progresso) Ongoing relationship building Não concentrar tudo em weekly calls   Reporting Systems: Triangulating the truth (3 fontes de dados) Ambas as partes devem confiar nos dados Three-tier system: Good, Great, Unacceptable Incluir todas as despesas no ROAS (fee, comissões, COGS) Offline conversion tracking CRM data Call tracking específico   Added Value Examples: Presentes personalizados baseados em hobbies Vídeos de boas-vindas personalizados Conteúdo extra não esperado Doações para causas do cliente Concessões estratégicas em timing Produtos físicos (Swannies glasses)   ESTRUTURA:   Client Retention Framework: Multi-year relationships como padrão 9 em 10 clientes retidos Deals ruins saem em 1-2 meses máximo Proativo em remover bad fits   Onboarding Timeline: Eight Sleep (negativo): June → July → parcial → 2 semanas depois James (positivo): 1 hora → 72 horas → 1 semana → 30 dias   Feedback Implementation: "1 pessoa dizendo = centenas pensando" Implementar SOPs baseados em feedback Gravações de aulas por demanda Biblioteca organizada de conteúdo Time-coded videos   Relationship Building: Perguntas sobre família, viagens, hobbies Piadas internas e conexões pessoais Presença em eventos do cliente Comunicação verbal regular (não só texto) Voice notes quando apropriado   DADOS FINANCEIROS: Eight Sleep: $6,652 total Cliente perdido: $1.5-2M/mês revenue → $100-200K/mês fee perdido Investimento em relacionamento: $1,000 doação para cliente $80-90K/mês Medical intervention: $700 (efetivo) vs $900 (inefetivo) Cost per call range: $45 (excelente) a $450 (bom para high ticket) a $900 (limite) ROI tiers example: 5:1 (great), 3:1 (good), 2:1 (unacceptable)   LIÇÕES CHAVE: Relacionamento pode superar resultados no longo prazo Comunicação inadequada perde clientes mesmo com ótimos resultados Valor adicionado deve ser inesperado para ter impacto Reporting deve ser mutuamente confiável Pequenas vitórias criam momentum para grande vitória Feedback de um representa feedback de muitos   DURAÇÃO:  ~97 minutos  FORMATO:  Masterclass ao vivo / Inner Circle teaching session
